items.q: What does the M+ peak in a mass spectrum represent?
items.correct: The molecular ion — formed when a single electron is removed from the whole molecule, giving the relative molecular mass.
items.misconception: The M+ peak shows the mass of the largest fragment after the molecule breaks apart.
items.random_wrong: The M+ peak shows the mass of a single hydrogen atom added to the molecule.
items.why: Students confuse the M+ peak (the parent ion at the molecular mass) with fragment peaks; they think the highest mass peak is always a fragment, not the intact ionised molecule.

items.q: What are the units of Kc for a reaction?
items.correct: They depend on Δn (the difference between moles of gaseous/aqueous products and reactants) — Kc can be dimensionless or have units like mol dm^-3.
items.misconception: Kc always has units of mol dm^-3.
items.why: Students learn Kc with a fixed unit example and don't realise units arise from the exponents in the expression; for A + B ⇌ C + D, Kc is unitless, but for A ⇌ B + C, Kc has units.

items.q: What is reduction in terms of electrons?
items.correct: Gain of electrons (the oxidation number is reduced).
items.misconception: Reduction means losing oxygen and oxidation means gaining oxygen — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Reduction means a substance becomes physically smaller in size.
items.why: Students learn the oxygen definition first (from rusting/combustion) and never update to the more general electron-transfer definition.

items.q: Sodium has atomic number 11. Its electron configuration is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2,8,1. “2,9” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An atom of Mg loses 2 electrons to form Mg²⁺. Its number of protons is:
items.why: It's 12. “10” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many neutrons in an atom with mass number 23 and atomic number 11?
items.why: Exact answer: 12 neutrons. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
